topics: Kyber: ultra-low latency video SDK for robotics and teleoperation
Next-generation codecs: AV2, H.266/VVC, and the patent landscape
Open source licensing and European tech entrepreneurship

claims.claim: VLC is being split into multiple sandboxed processes (decoding, de-muxing, filters) to improve security without sacrificing performance, analogous to Chrome's tab isolation.
evidence: VLC has around 500 plugins including FFmpeg, GPU drivers, and third-party codecs running inside the same process
The sandbox must sustain gigabits per second of memory copies, far exceeding typical web application demands
Comparison made to Chrome crashing a tab without crashing the whole browser

claims.claim: Kyber achieves 7 milliseconds glass-to-glass latency currently, with a target of 4 milliseconds, using a single QUIC/UDP socket for multiplexed audio, video, and control streams.
evidence: NVIDIA hardware encoder takes ~3.5ms, Intel decoder ~2ms, totaling ~6ms for codec alone
4 milliseconds corresponds to 240Hz refresh equivalent
Demo at CES showed a 3D-printed rover controlled from France over the internet

claims.claim: AV2 achieves approximately 30% bandwidth reduction over AV1 at equivalent quality, continuing the generational ~30% improvement trend across codec generations.
evidence: HEVC is ~30% better than H.264; H.266 is ~30% better than H.265
AV2 specification is complete and VLC demonstrated first AV2 demo at CES
The AV2 decoder is called DAV2D (David 2), a recursive acronym

claims.claim: HEVC licensing became commercially untenable due to fragmented patent pools, motivating large companies to create the royalty-free AV1/AV2 codecs via the Alliance for Open Media.
evidence: HEVC had multiple patent pools: MPEG-LA, HEVC Advance, Nokia separately outside pools
HP reportedly removed HEVC support from Windows laptops due to increasing patent costs
YouTube/Netflix licensing costs could reach hundreds of millions of dollars per year

claims.claim: Kyber uses forward error correction and clock drift compensation to ensure coherent multi-sensor data synchronization for robot AI training.
evidence: Over-transmitting a few percent of data allows packet loss recovery without adding latency
All robot sensors (cameras, GPS, captors) need synchronized timestamps for valid AI model training
Existing solutions work with one camera but become unreliable with five or more cameras

claims.claim: VLC's build process is intentionally air-gapped and paranoid, compiling on offline machines that bootstrap the compiler, with double signing, after detecting what they believe was a state actor attempting to inject a fake binary.
evidence: Compile boxes start by compiling the compiler and are never connected to the internet
A believed non-Western governmental agency attempted to push a fake binary to VLC's servers
Double signature process is used for release signing

claims.claim: Kyber is dual-licensed under AGPL and a commercial license, making it free for open-source projects but paid for proprietary commercial use.
evidence: If you use Kyber in a product, the full product must be open source under AGPL
Large companies pay for the commercial license to get support, IP rights, and modification rights
Described as same model referenced earlier in the conversation about licenses

quote_candidates.text: The goal of kyber is to make distance disappear because it is a projection of skills or projection of power.
quote_candidates.speaker: Jean-Baptiste Kempf

quote_candidates.text: 4 milliseconds means 240 hertz, right? Yeah, it's nuts.
quote_candidates.speaker: Jean-Baptiste Kempf

quote_candidates.text: There is no code that gets into VLC that we don't control.
quote_candidates.speaker: Jean-Baptiste Kempf

quote_candidates.text: If I had to pay all the license fee for VLC, I needed to pay more than 200 euros per user.
quote_candidates.speaker: Jean-Baptiste Kempf

quote_candidates.text: You shouldn't be able to patent math and matrices. Like this is wrong.
quote_candidates.speaker: Jean-Baptiste Kempf

quote_candidates.text: multimedia is what we call a patent minefield. There is two places where you have the most patents. It's everything related to 3G, 4G, 5G, RF, and multimedia
quote_candidates.speaker: Jean-Baptiste Kempf
